Title: Requiem
summary_P: Paris, the prince of Troy, lies alone in his extravagant bed, estranged from Helen and Oenone, the only ones he has ever loved being Hector, his brother. Hector, who loves and hates Paris simultaneously, confronts him for bringing Helen to Ilium, sparking war, and weakening his resolve. Despite their tumultuous relationship, Hector admits he cannot hate Paris entirely. After a passionate yet bitter encounter, Hector leaves, foreseeing his own death at Achilles' hands as punishment for killing Patroclus. The next day, Hector is slain by Achilles, and Paris, overcome with guilt and grief, vows revenge, seeking Apollo's aid to avenge his brother's death, acknowledging that Hector was the only person he ever truly loved.
Reason: No errors for Victim, Perpetrator, or Mode of Demise.

Title: what we can never say
summary_P: The narrative recounts Achilles' complex relationships and internal struggles during the Trojan War. In the spring of Year 10, Achilles is awarded Briseis as a prize and taunts her, but his true affection lies with Patroclus. Their bond deepens as they share intimate moments, while Briseis becomes a secondary figure in Achilles' life. By summer, tension arises as Patroclus grows unhappy, though their physical connection remains strong despite emotional distance. In autumn, Achilles is consumed by grief over Patroclus' death, unable to let go of his body and seeking solace in the idea of reuniting with him in the afterlife. By winter, Achilles defeats the Amazon Queen but is haunted by memories of Patroclus, whose beauty and significance overshadow all else. Throughout, Achilles' love for Patroclus endures as his defining force, even as he grapples with loss and glory.
Reason: Errors but no TPs.

Title: Intended
summary_P: The story revolves around the complex relationship between Achilles and Penthesilea, queen of the Amazons. Three years prior, they shared a deep connection, discussing fate and curses, with Achilles noting her wisdom and strength. Their interactions were marked by mutual respect and unspoken intimacy, as he avoided being addressed by name, even by lovers. Years later, they face each other in battle, recognizing each other beneath their warrior masks. The fight is intense, with both showcasing their prowess, but ultimately, Achilles strikes the fatal blow, killing Penthesilea. As he mourns her death, reflecting on their past and the inevitability of their fates, it becomes clear that neither deserved more than what destiny had ordained for them.

Title: The Face That—
summary_P: Helen of Sparta possesses a divine yet hollow beauty that lacks substance, making it inhuman and unfulfilling. Aphrodite, recognizing this emptiness, ignites passion in Paris for Helen, knowing she cannot inspire true emotional connection. Helen, treated as a prize by her husband and catalyst for war in Troy, finds unexpected kindness from Hector, who treats her with genuine decency and compassion. As the war rages on and Hector dies, Helen is devastated but later finds solace in Sparta with her children, easing her pain over time. Centuries later, Marlowe portrays her as a goddess-like figure, though Helen would have laughed at this if she could stop weeping. In the afterworld, her beauty becomes natural and peaceful, free from its once-dehumanizing effect, finally bringing her peace.
Reason: Errors in: Perpetrator (FP or FN), Mode of Demise (FP or FN) with at least one TP

Title: _______ π_________ __________ (Appearing Like Images of Dreams)
summary_P: Cassandra enters the palace with her eyes closed, guided by an unerring path, her mind consumed by visions of bloodshed and death, including Agamemnon's and her own, as well as the memory of Hades awaiting her. Clytaemnestra confronts her angrily, accusing her of being a harlot, and their exchange reveals Clytaemnestra's deep pain, anger, and madness over the years of waiting for her husband and the betrayal involving Helen. Cassandra, overwhelmed by these visions, laughs uncontrollably as she collapses, burdened by ten years of war and its impact on all three women—Cassandra, Clytaemnestra, and Helen. The narrative shifts to fragmented memories of Helen and Cassandra's past conversations, highlighting their emotional bond and Helen's conflicted feelings about leaving Sparta. Returning to the present, Clytaemnestra's rage intensifies; she violently attacks Cassandra, dragging her by the hair and slamming her against the wall. Cassandra remains detached, reflecting on how she and Helen are ultimately different, as Helen would never allow herself to be tainted by bloodshed. The story concludes with a vision of a woman's face—pale, bloodied, and hollow-eyed—a final, haunting image before Cassandra's death.
Reason: Errors in: Mode of Demise (FP or FN) with at least one TP

summary_P: Medusa, refusing Poseidon's advances, is pursued by him into the temple of Athena, where the sea's force devastates the city, causing floods and death. Her sisters Euryale and Stheno help survivors flee, but Medusa remains, overwhelmed by rage and grief. Athena transforms her into a monster with serpents for hair and a petrifying gaze after Medusa confronts her about injustice. Medusa destroys the city, leaving only statues behind. Title: Medusa
summary_P: Medusa, once a beautiful maiden cursed by Athena for Poseidon's actions, resides on the Island of the Gorgons with her sisters. Her gaze turns men to stone, and she has collected hundreds of statues, yearning for true love despite her isolation. Perseus arrives, using a golden shield as a mirror to locate her without direct eye contact. Medusa is intrigued by his cleverness and engages him in conversation. She envisions happiness with Apollo, their children, and a perfect son, but foresaw darkness: Paris's arrival, war, her sons dying, and her daughter being assaulted by Ajax in Athena's temple. Despite her grief and pregnancy, she tries to protect her family but ultimately sees Troy's fall, her own death, and the world's end with Apollo's rage. Knowing the futility of changing fate, she decides to hurt Apollo irrevocably, leading to his curse and her death, while he later feels hollow and seeks revenge.
Reason: Errors in: Victim (FP or FN) with at least one TP

summary_P: The narrator begins by hunting a rabbit and then attacks a beehive with an arrow to collect honeycombs, enduring stings in the process. The family enjoys the warm rabbit and bread together, expressing gratitude after finishing their meal.

Title: Not Her Story
summary_P: Briseis, a captive Trojan princess, loses her husband, city, and freedom in the war, becoming a slave to Achilles. Initially traumatized by her capture and enslavement, she finds some solace in Achilles' disciplined behavior toward her, though she recognizes their relationship is not one of love but coercion. Over time, she observes the deep bond between Achilles and his companion Patroclus, realizing their relationship is rooted in mutual passion and dependence, far exceeding a typical warrior's camaraderie. After Patroclus' death, Achilles descends into grief and vengeance, ultimately leading to his own death. Briseis reflects on how both men were victims of the war, with even Achilles, despite his fame, being consumed by it. She later shares their story, acknowledging her role as a mere footnote in their epic tale.
Reason: No errors for Victim, Perpetrator, or Mode of Demise.

Title: Cast Down but Not Destroyed
summary_P: Corrie Worth, a newly widowed woman and wife of the third-richest mine owner in Harlan County, prepares for Dixon Worth's funeral with careful attention to appearances, donning a black veil and gloves. She reflects on her humble beginnings, remembering how she met Dixon when he offered her peppermint candy as a starving girl. At the funeral, Sheriff J.H. Blair is confident he has arrested the right men for Dixon’s murder, but Corrie suspects otherwise. A young woman named Araminta Sizemore, who had an affair with Dixon and attempted suicide, mourns him quietly, exchanging a brief connection with Corrie. After discovering Dixon's infidelity through lipstick on his handkerchief, Corrie sent Araminta gloves with a hidden message. Haunted by nightmares of accused miners, Corrie plans to seek comfort from Blair after Dixon’s burial but will help the jailed miners escape by making Blair lose the jail keys temporarily. The story culminates in the revelation that Corrie killed Dixon with a pickaxe, showing her strength and resolve to end his abusive reign.
Reason: No errors for Victim, Perpetrator, or Mode of Demise.

Title: Witch, Goddess, and home again
summary_P: The man lies on an uncomfortable bed, feeling sorrow as he reluctantly joins his wife, though he pulls away when she touches him, only returning after her stern glance. He escapes into sleep but knows he'll face another island and goddess, this time unable to leave. Hermes arrives, and despite initial enthusiasm, the man recalls sea horrors, feeling it's better to stay than face what lies beyond. Home, the room is locked, the servant hanged, suitors dead, Penelope and Telemachus safe, bed unmoved. He rests on her lap, recounting war and sea tales but refusing to mention the Goddess or Witch. He flinches at her kiss, haunted by nightmares of those killed, some his fault. He remembers Hades, seeing fallen heroes and his mother, missing her deeply. His sister mourns Eurylochus, whose death pains him despite their animosity. Penelope comforts him, sees through his facade, believes in him, loves him more for her unwavering support, never asking anything in return. He feels she deserves better.
Reason: Errors in: Perpetrator (FP or FN) with at least one TP

Title: Heracles
summary_P: Prometheus refuses to wear an ivy wreath as a symbol of guilt, arguing it is beneath him. Heracles supports him by creating his own crude wreath from olive branches and placing the ivy wreath on Prometheus' head. Hermes demands Prometheus reveal the name of the goddess he protected, warning of dire consequences if he remains silent. Title: Heroides
summary_P: The speaker identifies as Baucis, a figure tied to the stories of Peleus, Achilles, and Odysseus, reflecting on her complex emotions toward Achilles—hating his resemblance to Peleus while loving him deeply. She recounts her role in attempting to disguise Achilles as a woman on Skyros, which ultimately failed. Baucis describes herself as a witch, unapologetic about fulfilling her purpose, and critiques societal expectations of women, asserting her independence and defiance against patriarchal systems. She also touches on themes of identity, power, and the enduring roles of women across history, from ancient Greece to modern times, while expressing pride in raising her son despite his flaws and questioning her own legacy.
Reason: Errors but no TPs.

Title: They Call Her Cassandra
summary_P: Riliane, a young princess with prophetic visions, is ostracized for her ability to foresee future events, which are dismissed as madness or nightmares. Her favorite myth, that of Cassandra, resonates deeply with her as she faces similar skepticism and isolation. Despite her visions saving her life multiple times, she is shamed and marginalized, eventually betrothed to limit her power. When her mother dies, Riliane mourns not the queen but her lost future and the estrangement of her brother. She sees the ministry's corruption leading to war and her own downfall, realizing her fate mirrors Cassandra's. During a revolution, she stands on a balcony, contemplating her willingness to endure further suffering, and ultimately jumps to her death, identifying with Cassandra's tragic end.

Title: Loved by the Sun
summary_P: Cassandra, a Trojan princess now enslaved, recounts her traumatic experiences after the fall of Troy. She reflects on being repeatedly assaulted by Agamemnon, who fears her wrath and the prophecy of his eventual doom. On a stormy sea voyage, she falls and injures herself, prompting Agamemnon to carry her below deck. Despite his attempts at intimacy, Cassandra remains emotionally detached, thinking of her destroyed city and deceased family. She contemplates her lost freedom, the futility of her prophecies, and the inevitable fall of Mycenae, as she endures Agamemnon's advances in silence, her spirit crushed but her memories of Troy enduring.

Title: Iphigenia's Love Song
summary_P: Iphigenia, moments before her sacrificial death, awakens in the Fields of Asphodel, greeted by Queen Persephone of the Underworld. The Fates—Clotho, Lachesis, and Atropos—are summoned to offer Iphigenia a chance to rewrite her fate. Clotho proposes altering her past, but Iphigenia refuses, unwilling to erase her family's future. Lachesis offers a different present, revealing a long, bloody war and eventual peace, but Iphigenia rejects this as well, angered by her own unjust death. Persephone then shows her the triumph of her family in the future, yet Iphigenia remains unfulfilled, expressing resentment over her sacrifice. Finally, Atropos offers revenge by manipulating events to bring about her father’s demise, which fills Iphigenia with satisfaction. She accepts this path, choosing vengeance over other alternatives.
Reason: No errors for Victim, Perpetrator, or Mode of Demise.

Title: The Demigod And The Gorgon
summary_P: Perball fails to kill the Gorgon after discovering the head he presented was a fake, filled with snakes that bite him, and plots his revenge. Returning to the temple with soldiers sent by Anithena, they face relentless rain and a mysterious statue before encountering the Gorgon inside. The Gorgon kills one soldier and defeats the others with her sword, but Perball uses a mirror shield to trick her into attacking it while he hides. When she approaches, he beheads her, only for the severed head to transform into a squealing piglet with fake snakes. The decapitated body reveals its true form, and a winged horse rescues the piglet. Clardusa, the Gorgon, heals her son Chyroar's wounds but is nearly attacked by an invisible Perball until Pegasus intervenes. Perball fights off Pegasus, injures it, and throws his sword at Clardusa, piercing her wing as she flees with Chyroar into the forest. Determined to finish her, Perball pursues them into the woods.
Reason: All three elements have FP or FN.

Title: Twice-Born
summary_P: Zeus, fearing for his lover Semele’s mortality, begged her not to demand he reveal himself in his full divine glory, warning that no mortal could withstand it. However, Semele, driven by the promise of immortality and guided by an old crone (likely Hera in disguise), insisted Zeus honor his oath sworn on the River Styx. Despite his warnings, she remained resolute, and Zeus reluctantly complied. His divine fire consumed her, leaving her lifeless with charred eye sockets, though their unborn son was protected in her womb. Grief-stricken, Zeus washed her body and performed a gruesome surgery to save their child, stitching the fetus into his thigh. The pain of this act caused earthquakes, tsunamis, and storms, but Zeus endured it, knowing the child, later called Dionysus the twice-born, would be powerful. After healing, Zeus ascended Mount Olympus, eager to see Hera’s reaction to his defiance.
